ServerSideEncrypted.^description: You have not enabled server side encryption (SSE) on {$COUNT} buckets which automatically encrypts objects uploaded to the bucket. If this bucket contains non-publically-available data, and you are not implementing client-side encryption, please enable SSE.

SSEWithKMS.^description: You have not enabled server side encryption (SSE) with KMS on {$COUNT} buckets which automatically encrypts objects uploaded to the bucket. If this bucket contains non-publically-available data, and you are not implementing client-side encryption, please enable SSE with KMS.

AccessControlList.^description: You are using {$COUNT} S3 buckets with ACLs. ACLs are legacy access control mechanisms that predate IAM. Instead of ACLs, we recommend using S3 bucket policies or AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) policies to manage access to your S3 buckets.

PublicAccessBlock.^description: You have disabled public access block on {$COUNT} S3 buckets.This leaves your data to be accessible by anyone. Unless absolutely necessary, enable public access block. Block Public Access at the S3 bucket level provides controls to ensure that objects never have public access. Public access is granted to buckets and objects through access control lists (ACLs), bucket policies, or both.

PublicReadAccessBlock.^description: You have allowed public read access on {$COUNT} S3 buckets. This leaves your bucket accessible by anyone. Unless absolutely necessary, enable public access block. Block Public Access at the S3 bucket level provides controls to ensure that objects never have public access. Public access is granted to buckets and objects through access control lists (ACLs), bucket policies, or both.

PublicWriteAccessBlock.^description: You have allowed public write access on {$COUNT} S3 buckets. This leaves your data to be accessible by anyone. Unless absolutely necessary, enable public access block. Block Public Access at the S3 bucket level provides controls to ensure that objects never have public access. Public access is granted to buckets and objects through access control lists (ACLs), bucket policies, or both.

MFADelete.^description: You have not enabled MFA delete on {$COUNT} buckets. MFA delete provides added security if, for example, your security credentials are compromised. MFA delete can help prevent accidental bucket deletions by requiring the user who initiates the delete action to prove physical possession of an MFA device with an MFA code and adding an extra layer of friction and security to the delete action.

BucketVersioning.^description: You have not enabled versioning on {$COUNT} buckets. Versioning in Amazon S3 is a means of keeping multiple variants of an object in the same bucket. You can use the S3 Versioning feature to preserve, retrieve, and restore every version of every object stored in your buckets.With versioning you can recover more easily from both unintended user actions and application failures.

ObjectLock.^description: You have not enabled object lock on {$COUNT} buckets. Object Lock can help prevent objects from being deleted or overwritten for a fixed amount of time or indefinitely.

BucketReplication.^description: You have not enabled bucket replication on {$COUNT} buckets. Replication enables automatic, asynchronous copying of objects across Amazon S3 buckets.

CrossRegionReplication.^description: You have not enabled cross-region replication on {$COUNT} buckets. Replication enables automatic, asynchronous copying of objects across Amazon S3 buckets.

EventNotification.^description: When you enable S3 Event Notifications, you receive alerts when specific events occur that impact your S3 buckets. For example, you can be notified of object creation, object removal, and object restoration. These notifications can alert relevant teams to accidental or intentional modifications that may lead to unauthorized data access.

BucketLifecycle.^description: You have not configured lifecycle policies for objects in {$COUNT} buckets. Lifecycle configuration is a set of rules that define actions that Amazon S3 applies to a group of objects. This will save you cost by moving infrequently accessed objects to lower cost storage tiers and expiring objects that are no longer needed.

BucketLogging.^description: You have not enabled server access logging in {$COUNT} buckets. Server access logging provides detailed records for the requests that are made to a bucket.

ObjectsInIntelligentTier.^description: Your objects in {$COUNT} S3 buckets are not in S3 Intelligent Tier. The S3 Intelligent-Tiering storage class is designed to optimize storage costs by automatically moving data to the most cost-effective access tier when access patterns change. For a small monthly object monitoring and automation charge, S3 Intelligent-Tiering monitors access patterns and automatically moves objects that have not been accessed to lower-cost access tiers. Unless all your objects are very frequently accessed, or the data lifecycle is very clearly known and defined, it is considered best practice to store your objects in Intelligent Tier.

TlsEnforced.^description: You have not enforced encryption of data in transit in {$COUNT} buckets. You can use HTTPS (TLS) to help prevent potential attackers from eavesdropping on or manipulating network traffic using person-in-the-middle or similar attacks. You should allow only encrypted connections over HTTPS (TLS) using the aws:SecureTransport condition on Amazon S3 bucket policies..
TlsEnforced.shortDesc: Enforce Encryption of Data in Transit

S3AccountPublicAccessBlock.^description: You have disabled public access block on your account.This allows your buckets and onjects to be potentially made public. Unless absolutely necessary, enable public access block at the account level.

MacieToEnable.^description: You should evaluate using a tool, such as Amazon Macie, that uses machine learning to automatically discover, classify, and protect sensitive data in AWS. Amazon Macie recognizes sensitive data, such as personally identifiable information (PII) or intellectual property, and provides you with dashboards and alerts that give visibility into how this data is being accessed or moved.

BucketOwnerEnforced.^description: You have not enabled 'Bucket Owner Enforced' setting on {$COUNT} buckets. This setting disables ACLs and ensures that the bucket owner automatically owns and has full control over every object in the bucket. ACLs are legacy access control mechanisms that can create security vulnerabilities and are difficult to audit at scale. AWS recommends disabling ACLs by enabling 'Bucket Owner Enforced' for all buckets.

TransferAcceleration.^description: You have not enabled Transfer Acceleration on {$COUNT} buckets. Transfer Acceleration enables fast, easy, and secure transfers of files over long distances between your client and an S3 bucket by leveraging Amazon CloudFront's globally distributed edge locations. This is particularly beneficial for applications with geographically distributed users uploading or downloading large files.

CloudWatchRequestMetrics.^description: You have not enabled CloudWatch request metrics on {$COUNT} buckets. CloudWatch request metrics provide visibility into S3 bucket operations, including request counts, latency, and error rates. These metrics enable proactive monitoring, security incident detection, cost optimization, and compliance auditing. Enabling request metrics helps identify unusual access patterns and supports operational troubleshooting.

WildcardPrincipalsActions.^description: You have {$COUNT} buckets with wildcard principals ('Principal': '*') or wildcard actions ('Action': '*' or 's3:*') in Allow statements of their bucket policies. This is a critical security risk that grants overly permissive access and violates the principle of least privilege. Wildcard principals allow anyone on the internet to access your bucket, and wildcard actions grant all S3 permissions including destructive operations. This is a common source of data breaches and unauthorized access. Review and restrict these policies to specific principals and actions, or use Condition clauses to mitigate risks.

SSECBlocking.^description: You have {$COUNT} buckets that do not block SSE-C (Server-Side Encryption with Customer-Provided Keys). AWS recommends using SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS instead of SSE-C due to operational complexity and key management risks. With SSE-C, customers must manage encryption keys themselves, and key loss means permanent data loss with no AWS recovery option. Blocking SSE-C through bucket policies and using AWS-managed encryption simplifies operations, reduces risk, and improves compliance posture.

StorageLens.^description: S3 Storage Lens is not enabled for your AWS account. Storage Lens provides organization-wide visibility into S3 storage usage, activity trends, and cost optimization opportunities. It helps identify unused buckets, optimize storage classes, track storage growth, and improve security posture. The free tier includes basic metrics, while advanced metrics provide additional insights. Enabling Storage Lens helps reduce costs and improve operational efficiency.

PublicAccessDocumentation.^description: You have {$COUNT} public buckets without proper approval documentation. While public access may be intentional for some use cases (e.g., static website hosting), all public buckets should have documented approval through tags such as 'PublicAccessApproved=true' and 'ApprovalDate'. This ensures public access is intentional, provides an audit trail, and enables periodic review of justifications. Undocumented public buckets may indicate accidental exposure or lack of governance.

UnpredictableBucketNames.^description: You have {$COUNT} buckets with predictable naming patterns. AWS recommends using unpredictable bucket names to reduce the risk of unauthorized access attempts through enumeration. Predictable patterns include sequential numbers, dates, common words, or obvious naming conventions. While this is a defense-in-depth measure and proper access controls are more important, unpredictable names add an additional layer of security by making it harder for attackers to guess bucket names.
